data.title: Retire With Long-Term Investment Grade Bonds (Part 1): W. R. Berkley
data.description:
In today's article, we’ll look at W. R. Berkley Corporation (NYSE:WRB) company's key financials, its bonds, and how they compare to others in the sector.

Overview

W. R. Berkley Corporation is a company that operates in the insurance and reinsurance sector. Its total assets at the end of the first quarter of 2025 are close to $42 billion. The current market capitalization of WRB is around $28 billion. The company was founded in 1967 and is headquartered in Greenwich, Connecticut, United States. Currently, the common stock of the company is at a market price of $74.20 and below you can see the comparison with SPY's total return for the last 5 years:

Dividends

WRB has 35 consecutive years of uninterrupted dividend payments, with the last 19 years of them being consecutive dividend increases. This is what the dividend history of the company looks like:

The twelve-month trailing dividend distributed to WRB's common shareholders amounts to $1.40. With the current price of the stock, that corresponds to a dividend yield of 1.44%. Below, you can observe the dividend yield for the past 5 years:

The 10-year dividend growth rate is 10.35%, which is a solid percentage compared to the sector median, but is probably due to the small starting point anyway.

The payout ratio is only 7.79%, one of the lowest in the sector.

This is one of the reasons that dividend investors should consider the company's bonds.

Credit rating

Below, you can see how the credit companies rate the senior debt and the subordinated debt of the company:

For all companies, it falls under the investment grade category. It is no coincidence that the company is highly rated by credit companies, with an established market position in specialized insurance and proven experience in stable profitability and good capital adequacy.

Baby Bonds

WRB has four exchange-traded securities:

NYSE:WRB.PR.E is trading at $21.64 at the moment. It pays 5.70% interest distributions per annum in the form of $0.35625 quarterly, and a $1.425 annual amount. Its maturity date is on 03/30/2058, with close to 33 years to maturity, and its call date was on 03/30/2023, so it is callable anytime. WRB issued 7 million notes of WRB.PR.E, each with a par value of $25 on 03/20/2018.

At the time of writing, NYSE:WRB.PR.G is trading at $16.24. It pays 4.25% annual interest in the form of $0.265625 per quarter, $1.0625 per annum. Its maturity date is on 09/30/2060, with close to 35 years to maturity, and its call date is on 09/30/2025, with 0.36 years to call. The company issued 10 million notes of WRB.PR.G, each with a par value of $25, on 09/16/2020.

NYSE:WRB.PR.H's current market price is $15.69. It pays 4.13% annual interest in the form of $0.2578125 per quarter, $1.03125 per annum. Its maturity date is on 03/30/2061, with close to 36 years to maturity, and its call date is on 03/30/2026, with around 1 year to call. WRB issued 12 million notes of WRB.PR.H, each with a par value of $25 on 02/03/2021.

NYSE:WRB.PR.F is trading at $19.33 at the moment. It pays 5.10% interest distributions per annum in the form of $0.31875 quarterly, and a $1.275 annual amount. Its maturity date is on 12/30/2059, with close to 35 years to maturity, and its call date was on 12/20/2024, so it is callable anytime. W. R. Berkley Corporation issued 12 million notes of WRB.PR.F, each with a par value of $25 on 12/03/2019.

All four are rated by Moody's with a Baa2 credit rating and by S&P with a BBB- rating.

OTC Bonds

On the chart above are the OTC-traded bond issues of W. R. Berkley Corporation with their corresponding metrics. All of them are rated by Moody's with a Baa1 credit rating, or a notch higher than the baby bonds. The baby bonds still look undervalued with a 1% higher yield, compared to the senior notes, even after adjusting the OTCs with a 0.25% higher risk premium, to compare at fair value. You can see the bond yield curve of the company below in the chart:

Comparison With Peers

Below in the chart, you can see all baby bonds in the Insurance sector, which are investment grade and trade below par:

Compared to bonds that trade over the counter, our baby bonds have a higher yield, even than the highest yields for the sector:

Conclusion

WRB has a strong credit rating and a stable profitability. While its common stock offers only modest returns, the company's securities, particularly its baby bonds, are a compelling option, offering significantly higher risk premiums than OTC bonds, even after adjusting for credit quality differences. We have nothing bad to say about the company, except for its dividend payout ratio.

data.title: Tariff Turmoil Clouds European Investment Banks' Advisory, Underwriting Outlook
data.description:
Tariff-induced volatility is expected to dampen second-quarter advisory and underwriting revenue at most of Europe's largest investment banks.

Barclays PLC (BCS), BNP Paribas SA (OTCQX:BNPQF)(OTCQX:BNPQY), Société Générale SA (OTCPK:SCGLF)(OTCPK:SCGLY), HSBC Holdings PLC (HSBC) and UBS Group AG (UBS) will post weaker revenue in the segment for the second quarter compared to the previous three-month period, according to consensus estimates from Visible Alpha, a part of S&P Global Market Intelligence.

Deutsche Bank AG (DB) is the only one among the continent's six leading players forecast to book a revenue increase, thanks to stronger debt origination, which accounted for over 60% of its advisory and underwriting activities in the first quarter.

Investment banking fees estimates at large European banks, 2025

Deutsche Bank AG

BNP Paribas SA

Barclays PLC

Société Générale SA

HSBC Holdings PLC

UBS Group AG

Data compiled May 23, 2025.

e = estimates.

Investment banking fees represent the sum of advisory and capital markets income. Data represents Visible Alpha consensus estimates as of May 23, 2025, based on at least three analyst contributions.

Division results vary according to business mix and may not be directly comparable from bank to bank.

Actual data combines company reported numbers with the intelligence of sell-side community to provide actuals that are likely to be most comparable with their forecasts. Source: Visible Alpha, a part of S&P Global Market Intelligence.

© 2025 S&P Global.

Uncertainty related to US tariffs and their impact on markets, monetary policy and economic growth has dimmed the prospects for a recovery in dealmaking and IPO activity this year. Equity underwriting and advisory estimates have been trending downward since the beginning of 2025, Visible Alpha analysts said in a May 9 note.

"The outlook has become more difficult for senior leaders to predict, likely putting deals and transactions on hold," the analysts said.

Debt underwriting, on the other hand, has been relatively steady, which could be a sign of underlying stability in market conditions and borrowers' creditworthiness, according to the analysts.

Soon after the tariff announcements in early April, analyst consensus estimates for advisory and equity underwriting fees at the largest five US investment banks — Bank of America Corp. (BAC), Citigroup Inc. (C), Goldman Sachs Group Inc. (GS), JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) and Morgan Stanley (MS) — were also downgraded, Visible Alpha data compiled by Market Intelligence shows. In contrast, debt underwriting projections were upgraded.

Banks hopeful

In the first quarter, advisory and underwriting revenue increased across most major European and US investment banks compared to a year ago, data compiled by Market Intelligence shows.

Based on filings of banks that report revenue by business separately, advisory and debt underwriting largely increased year-over-year, while equity underwriting revenue fell at most banks.

Deutsche Bank and UBS, which booked the largest year-over-year declines, are both hopeful of an improvement later in the year.

"While the market environment weighed on our [investment] banking results across products and regions, and despite growing economic uncertainty, our pipeline continues to build," UBS CFO Todd Tuckner said during the group's first-quarter earnings call on April 30.

UBS was dragged down by a drop in underwriting revenue, largely driven by weaker leverage finance business. Equity underwriting was also weaker, but the bank is focused on boosting its pipeline, which should "yield meaningful returns over the medium term," Tuckner said.

Deutsche Bank CEO Christian Sewing also spoke of a "robust pipeline" in the group's advisory and origination (O&A) business during its latest earnings call, projecting "healthy numbers" for the second and third quarter.

"We see some delayed deals, but no deal is canceled," Sewing said.

Deutsche Bank's first-quarter O&A revenue decline was due to a weaker debt underwriting result related to "a loss on the partial sale and markdown of a specific loan" in the group's leveraged finance business, CFO James von Moltke said.

Advisory slowdown

Compared to the previous quarter, overall revenue at most banks was lower in the first three months of 2025, primarily due to weaker advisory. Bank of America, Barclays, Deutsche Bank, Goldman Sachs, JPMorgan, Morgan Stanley and UBS all posted quarter-over-quarter revenue drops in that line of business, the data shows.

Earlier hopes for a dealmaking boom were quashed by "the reality of tariff-driven uncertainty, still-elevated interest rates and choppy equity markets," JPMorgan said in its 2025 midyear outlook report. Public market activity, notably the IPO space, has been very quiet, and projections for M&A activity growth have been cut in recent months, the bank said.

While there were some green shoots with global M&A activity rising 17% year-over-year in the first quarter, "the prospect of prolonged uncertainty has created a cloudier outlook on the pace for dealmaking, at least over the near term," JPMorgan said.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Paccar: Hold On For Now
data.description:
Company Description

Paccar Inc. (NASDAQ:PCAR) is a leading producer and distributor of commercial trucks and parts. The company has three reportable segments; Truck, Parts, and Financial Services. PCAR employs more than 30,000 people worldwide and has a market cap just shy of $50B. Paccar has a 10-year total return of 214% which equates to a CAGR of 12.1%, essentially in-line with the broad market.

Quality Financial Metrics

Personally, I believe there are three key financial characteristics all high-quality businesses should possess: rising revenue, a stable gross profit margin, and a robust return on invested capital. Let's take a look at these metrics for PCAR and assess its performance.

The revenue per share is basically the same chart, just shown in 5-year increments. Looking at 2015 through 2019, the RPS dipped initially from FY15 to FY16, before rising at a solid rate over the next few years. The pandemic caused an obvious slowdown, but again, in the years that followed the RPS grew at more than 20% in 2021 and 2022. There was a minor dip in FY24 of 4.4%, which is something investors should monitor, to ensure it doesn't continue to slip.

The gross profit margin for PCAR fluctuated from a low of 12.3% in FY20, but rebounded nicely over the next few years, peaking at 19.8% in FY23. Again though, there was a small decline (60 basis points) in FY24 which could be influenced by the normal economic cycle, production costs, and/or demand for PCAR's products.

The return on invested capital metric follows a similar path as the revenue per share. A decline from 2015 to 2016, before improvements over the next two fiscal years. The pandemic sank this metric before impressive increases through FY23. PCAR has shown it's capable of approaching my threshold of 20%, so it'll be interesting to see if this can be eclipsed in the future.

Dividend Data

Paccar is unique in that it pays a consistently growing quarterly dividend and then issues fluctuating annual dividends every December. Based on the dividend hike announced earlier this year in February (10%), coupled with the annual dividend in December 2024, PCAR currently yields 4.55%. Additionally, the 10-year dividend growth rate is an impressive 7.4%, with varying degrees of dividend growth along the way, as indicated by the blue line below. And lastly, the payout ratio has been on a downward trend over the past few years and in FY24 came in at just 12%, leaving plenty of room for future dividend growth.

Q1 2025 Earnings

In late April, Paccar announced their Q1 2025 earnings, Non-GAAP EPS was $1.46 per share, missing estimates by $0.12, on revenue of $7.44B, which beat the consensus by $350M but was a nearly 15% decrease YoY. Additionally, GAAP EPS for the quarter was $0.96 per share, which came up $0.63 short of expectations.

Taking a look at the company by segment, Truck revenue decreased 20%, dropping from $6.5B in Q1 2024 to just $5.2B in the most recent quarter. On that revenue, the Truck segment had a pretax profit of $364.9M, a more than 50% decline from Q1 2024. In the earnings presentation, management mentioned these numbers were affected greatly by the economic uncertainty surrounding the ongoing tariffs.

On a better note, the Parts segment had revenue increase nearly 1% to $1.68B from $1.67B, but pretax profit for the segment shrank by about 6% to $426.5M. However, the gross margin for this segment has been on a steady upward trend, rising from about 27% in FY15, to almost 31% in Q1 2025.

Revenue for the Financial Services portion of the business was $528M for the quarter, rising 3.5% compared to the same quarter a year ago.

The earnings report was rather mute for the stock, with a very minor slip following the release. The full earnings call can be read/listened to here.

Valuation

The last consideration is to determine if PCAR is trading for an attractive valuation and as always, we'll look at it from a free cash flow perspective. Additionally, to give a little more context on how to view the chart below, the estimated fair value is calculated by dividing the FCF/share for that year by the trailing 5-year average of the FCF yield. The discount line is 10% below the estimated FV, while the premium is 10% above, and finally, the Trim line is 40% above the approximate FV. Finally, you'll see the chart goes slightly into 2025, and because no complete 2025 FCF/share data is available the TTM FCF per share is used.

The custom valuation model suggests PCAR is trading in-line with its fair value at about $94 per share. The free cash flow was fairly stable until 2021 where it declined by more than half to $1.06, from $2.59 in 2020. Since then, the FCF has grown rather quickly, with dramatic increases in FY22 and FY23. In 2024, the FCF came in at a respectable $4.49, compared to $5.59 and $5.51 the prior two years.

The long-term expected rate of return for Paccar comes in essentially flat. As previously mentioned, the company has a current dividend yield of about 4.55% and a projected EPS growth rate of -4.22%.

Closing Thoughts

Overall, PCAR has performed well over the past decade, keeping up with the broad market. The financial metrics have been pretty solid; however, each of the three I touched on earlier all showed a decline in the most recent fiscal year, which investors should monitor. The dividend is very safe and has grown at a relatively healthy pace over the past ten years. The tariff wars have caused some issues for PCAR, as indicated in the recent earnings release, again this is something investors should keep an eye on. And finally, the valuation appears to make sense for PCAR at this time; however, the weak expected rate of return gives me pause, and thus I rate this stock a hold, but am hopeful the company will return to more consistent growth in the coming years.

data.title: BYD's Ultra-Fast Chargers Will Supercharge Growth While Price Wars Could Sink Rivals
data.description:
Thesis: BYD's Super e-Platform should open up new revenue streams and enable the company to build a large, profitable infrastructure. Other developments, including international expansion and recent price cuts, could further supercharge BYD's growth.

BYD (BYDDF)(BYDDY) is ushering in the Megawatt [MW] era of EVs, with their new Super e-Platform offering double the charging speeds of Tesla’s 500 kW superchargers. The new Super e-Platform can deliver roughly 250 miles of range in about five minutes. In other words, the new EV system offers a refueling experience similar to filling up a gas tank for a combustion vehicle.

Even Tesla’s 500 kW chargers were the exception, not the norm. Charging stations typically support 200-400 kW charging, meaning BYD can now leave the competition in the dust. Charging speeds are likely only to accelerate in the future, which will make EVs all the more attractive for potential buyers.

Meanwhile, BYD is moving forward with aggressive plans to expand internationally, often by setting up local manufacturing facilities, thus allowing the company to get around trade barriers. BYD has also moved aggressively to slash prices, in some cases by up to 34%

The Five-Minute Charging Time Is A Game Changer

Modern electric power trains typically offer great acceleration and torque. Charging a car at home or the office parking lot, meanwhile, is more convenient than heading to a gas station. Yet, many would-be consumers have been wary of purchasing an EV owing to limited range and slow charging times. (In China, EV adoption has already been strong, accounting for more than half of sales in 2024.)

A five-minute charging time changes the dynamics. A traditional combustion vehicle, for now, may still be a better choice for long road trips. However, most people make only occasional long road trips. The convenience offered by an EV, which can be charged at home, could quickly outweigh the inconvenience during occasional long trips, especially with 5-minute charging times.

Further, BYD's blade batteries seek to increase safety and longevity, contributing to the company's technological strength.

Infrastructure Investments offer BYD a Strong Path for Growth

BYD is looking to build 4,000 ultra-fast chargers across China, and it wouldn’t be a surprise if they expand into overseas markets as well. In China, BYD will be able to charge for charging services, with fees regulated based on electricity costs. Until now, BYD owners have typically had to rely on other automakers' charging stations or public charging poles.

China uses one national charging standard: GB/T. The Chinese government has sought to encourage open access to charging EVs. I haven't yet confirmed this, but I believe BYD's charging stations will be open to other auto brands, and the company may be encouraged to license its technologies to other manufacturers.

CCP Commitment Relieves Some Long-Term Risks

BYD is emerging as one of the most well-known Chinese brands, mounting a substantial challenge to Tesla in the global EV market as well as traditional automakers, including Toyota and General Motors. Many non-Chinese automakers are being pushed out of China, struggling to keep pace with dropping prices and the rapid shift to EVs.

The rapid rise of China’s EV industry has been fueled by investments by the Chinese government. The U.S.-based Center for Strategic and International Studies conducted a study and found that the Chinese government spent north of $230 billion from 2009 to 2023 to fuel the EV industry. Keep in mind, a dollar goes much further in China than in the United States.

Consider that Tesla’s R&D spending in 2024 weighed in at “only” $4.54 billion, a respectable amount in and of itself, but a rather paltry sum compared to how much China is spending. China is now arguably the world leader in EVs, and it’s a safe bet that the CCP will continue to offer substantial support. It’s going to be hard for foreign automakers to keep pace.

In response to the ongoing trade war, the Chinese government is seeking to spur local consumption and reduce dependency on overseas markets. The Chinese government is looking to subsidize childcare, leaving locals with more cash in their pockets to spend. The Chinese government is also aiming to boost wages at a "reasonable" rate. These efforts could drive people in China to BYD showrooms by affording them more discretionary income.

Overseas Markets Will Offer BYD Ample Opportunities

Increasingly, BYD is looking to expand its presence in overseas markets, which matches with the Chinese government’s goal to raise the prestige of China and its industries on the world stage.

Countries with large domestic automobile manufacturing industries, including not just the United States, South Korea, Japan, and many European Union members but also Thailand, Malaysia, and the like, may move to protect their markets. The United States, among others, is seeking to protect its domestic industry with high trade barriers, which does create some headwinds. This doesn't mean BYD will be locked out of these markets, but may instead be encouraged to make local investments.

At the same time, countries without substantial automobile industries have little incentive to keep BYD vehicles out of the market or to make them expensive via tariffs and other barriers. Access to such markets should prove easier for BYD.

Further, BYD can localize production in key markets, strengthening their local position. In 2024, BYD expected to sell 50,000 vehicles in Mexico, and in 2025 the company is aiming to sell 100,000. BYD is also moving to set up local production facilities in Mexico.

In another example, Thailand has a strong automobile industry, with Ford and others producing cars locally. Thailand’s government has long protected the local automobile industry, encouraging foreign companies to set up local production if they want full access to the market. BYD opened a production facility in July 2024 in Thailand and within four months had produced its 10,000th car. The facility will be able to produce 150,000 vehicles per year at full capacity. BYD’s investments have produced roughly 10,000 jobs locally, which is crucial for Thai lawmakers.

Given China’s contracting population, encouraging localized production makes economic and geopolitical sense for the CCP. And, of course, it makes business sense for BYD, allowing them to diversify and potentially control costs in the long run. Foreign investments also give both BYD and the Chinese government more leverage in target countries.

Still, BYD will face headwinds in some global markets. Particularly, wealthy markets in the United States, Europe, and across Asia may remain closed off or at least protected behind trade barriers. China has been criticized for trade practices and extensive industry support, with critics arguing that it amounts to unfair advantages. This may be true, but regardless, China is now the global leader in EVs.

Price Cuts Set To Shake Up The Market

China's EV market has been growing intensely competitive, and we're likely to see a few companies get pushed out of the market in the not-so-distant future. The biggest players, like BYD, may be relatively safe. Indeed, BYD may have just kicked off a price war, having recently slashed prices by up to 34% in China.

The price cuts may hint at improved efficiency and reduced costs. However, industry analysts suspect that BYD may be struggling to hit sales targets. Thus, the price cuts may be in response to sluggish sales, and BYD's margins could come under intense pressure. Yet, BYD's new charging technologies and international expansion both demonstrate a company with a long-term vision. So long as BYD doesn't stretch itself too thin and avoids costly mistakes, I believe the company has a bright future.

BYD Valuation And Fundamentals

American stocks have gotten rather expensive over the last few years. P/E ratios that would have been tough to swallow six or seven years ago are now the norm. Stocks in China, however, don't seem as prone to overvaluation, and BYD's shares are enticing. BYD's P/E GAAP [TTM] weighs in at "just" 25.18, not exactly cheap, but lower than most Magnificent 7 stocks (Meta is also at ~25, Google has consistently stayed below 20, the others are above 30).

A reverse DCF calculation also shows great value. Using GuruFocus's DCF calculator set with an EPS measurement and tangible book value added in, I find that to justify current prices, BYD needs to secure EPS growth of 10.66% to justify its current price. Switching to a FCF calculation, FCF growth will have to hit 21.25%, a substantially harder level to hit, but given that BYD is only now starting to rapidly expand globally, I believe these numbers are obtainable in the long run. Both calculations set the discount rate at the standard 10% and the terminal growth rate at 4% (in line with global GDP growth).

Keep in mind that the above calculations don't include the potential for BYD's rapid charging technologies. Of course, we can't count our eggs before they hatch, but charging infrastructure may offer an exciting new avenue for growth and profits.

Glancing through Seeking Alpha's data, a few other things stand out:

  • The Net Income Margin [TTM] is a healthy 5.45%.
  • The company has over $21 billion in cash and short-term investments, and nearly $115.78 billion in total assets.
  • Current liabilities weigh in at just under $70 billion, and total liabilities amount to $81.9 billion.
  • Annual revenue growth [YOY] topped 35% during the last reporting.

All in all, BYD's fundamentals strike me as strong. Expanding internationally will probably mean taking on more liabilities, but will also add assets and could pave the way for even stronger growth in the future. The same can be said for building up an expansive charging infrastructure.

Seeking Alpha's quant tools aren't as bullish, giving BYD a hold. Based on current data, this is a reasonable position. However, I believe the potential of future developments warrants an upgrade.

BYD Looks Like A Good Bet

BYD strikes me as a solid buy. BYD’s stock has enjoyed strong growth in recent days, but as a long-term investment, there’s still plenty of room to grow. BYD has enjoyed a lot of success as of late, and in the long run, they could enjoy plenty of sustained momentum. While the price war will hurt margins through the near future, BYD should be able to absorb the losses while smaller rivals struggle. Charging infrastructure may also offer new growth avenues.

China’s domestic market is large enough to sustain success, but what will be most interesting is how successful (or unsuccessful) BYD is in expanding to other markets. Given the ability of BYD and other Chinese manufacturers to undercut Korean, American, Japanese, and European automakers, I suspect they’ll find plenty of success, although they may have a harder time penetrating some key markets, like the United States.

Expect BYD to remain a centerpiece of China’s efforts to raise the overall prestige of China’s industries. This suggests that the Chinese government will provide support if needed or if it could further the CCP’s goals. That said, such support is a double-edged blade, and the Chinese government has shown a willingness to subjugate private industry ambitions to the long-term goals of the CCP, thus creating uncertainty.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: WiseTech Global: Solid Moat And Growth Potential
data.description:
Investment action

I give a buy rating for WiseTech Global (OTCPK:WTCHF)(OTCPK:WIGBY) as I believe WTCHF has a very strong moat, and its growth story is clear. On top of that, the e2open acquisition gives WTCHF access to new functions and greatly expands its ability to monetize. If things play out as I expected, I think WTCHF is well-positioned to deliver >20% annual revenue growth.

Company description

WTCHF is a logistics software provider best known for CargoWise. CargoWise is its core platform that helps freight forwarders and logistics operators streamline operations (tracking assets, calculating cost, finding freight, managing compliance, etc.). WTCHF’s customer base includes some of the world’s largest third-party logistics providers.

Competitive Strengths and Growth Outlook

WTCHF has a really strong market position in my view, and one that is secured by its solid competitive advantage. The key reason is how deeply CargoWise is embedded into customers’ operations. For new readers to WTCHF, CargoWise isn’t just a basic logistics tool—it is akin to the ERP/operating system of many customers that enables them to run day-to-day workflows like freight forwarding, customs, warehousing, and transport management. It’s like using Microsoft to function in typical office work. Once a customer is onboard, switching becomes extremely difficult. This is not just because of cost but because switching carries substantial operational risks, as there are just too many interconnected processes (both internal and external—like with customs).

Even better, WTCHF has invested strongly in continuously evolving CargoWise, and the reason it is able to evolve so well is because it is built on a single global codebase. This is another advantage I think WTCHF has vs. competitors that build their products via patching multiple point solutions together. These competing solutions do not have the same flexibility as CargoWise. For example, with the same research and development [R&D] dollar, CargoWise is more efficient since it only needs to update one codebase, vs. some peers that need to update multiple codebases (and hope that it works well across them). WTCHF spends around 33% of revenue consistently on R&D, and with each new module added, this not only raises the bar for competitors, but more importantly, it increases the switching costs for its customers.

As for the growth outlook, I think it is very positive. My view is that the logistics industry will continue to ramp up investments in software to make their lives easier as they need more speed, visibility, and control, and WTCHF is in a very good spot to capitalize on this trend because it is the “operating system.” It makes a lot of sense for customers to just adopt the solutions WTCHF offers since they are well integrated and work well. Remember, this is an industry that has multiple stakeholders and many moving parts involved. You don’t want to mess up a certain linkage and screw up the entire freight forwarding process.

Hence, WTCHF just needs to keep doing what it’s already doing well—rolling out new modules and expanding the CargoWise ecosystem. The more it does this, the stronger its moat becomes. Every time it connects a new piece of the supply chain, the platform becomes more valuable to everyone using it, and this creates a strong network effect. The more deeply integrated it is across global logistics, the harder it becomes for users to switch out.

The recent/upcoming launches of next-gen solutions—like ComplianceWise (launched in 1Q25) and Container Transport Optimization [CTO] (release in 2H25)—are good examples of WTCHF expanding its ecosystem. To give a simple idea of what these are: ComplianceWise is like a super helper that makes sure all the rules and paperwork are perfectly followed so clients don’t miss it. This is important because non-compliance results in fines, and in the worst case, revocation of license. As for CTO, it's like a really smart map for the containers. Basically, it finds the best, fastest, and cheapest way for these containers to travel, and this is important because it helps save money for customers.

Transformative e2open Merger

I believe the $2.1 billion acquisition of e2open is a game changer for WTCHF. This deal not only expands WTCHF's scale, it also expands the WTCHF ecosystem into critical functions like supply chain planning, domestic logistics, global trade compliance, and carrier connectivity. In simpler terms, these are more upstream functions (not the core strength of WTCHF with its software/infrastructure focus). While I like to think WTCHF should R&D all these functions themselves, I am in favor of this strategic decision since it significantly increases the time-to-market for WTCHF (management estimates this move accelerates their long-term roadmap by about a decade). Moreover, there is minimal overlap in either product set or customer base, and the combined entity will have a network of 500,000 businesses (including major ocean carriers and 250+ blue-chip shippers). This ultimately creates a wider, deeper, and interconnected network that gives WTCHF more nodes to monetize in its ecosystem.

From a financial perspective, this deal is immediately earnings accretive. For reference, e2open delivered $607.7 million in revenue and $215.5 million in adj. EBITDA for FY25 (ending Feb.), and this is ~60% of WTCHF FY24 revenue and 35% of FY24 adj. EBITDA. From an EPS standpoint, management commented that the transaction will be accretive to EPS in the first year, even before synergies, and has guided to $50 million in annualized run-rate cost savings by year two. I wouldn’t doubt the synergies available, since there are bound to be duplicated functions. In terms of funding, I believe WTCHF made the right decision to fully fund it with debt, since its balance sheet has plenty of debt headroom—post-deal, net leverage is ~3.5x by 4Q25—and given that WTCHF and e2open are cash generative, there is a clear path to deleveraging over the next few years.

Valuation

If the deal goes through smoothly, I think the upside is very attractive from here, as the combined entity should easily sustain >20% growth for the foreseeable future—driven by secular tailwinds, cross-selling, and new module rollouts. WTCHF was already growing at >20% pre-deal, so I don’t think it is impossible for it to continue. While e2open growth is not as great, I believe growth should accelerate significantly as WTCHF cross-sells e2open products into its existing ecosystem. The combined entity should also see margin expansion due to cost synergies. Because of the higher leverage ratio, I don’t think multiples will move up. WTC is already trading at its historical average of 42x forward EBITDA, and assuming this stays the same, I see a potential for the stock to double in 3 years.

Risk

While I see the logic behind the e2open deal, the integration is not going to be easy. e2open itself is a roll-up with many products under its belt. Aligning all of them with the WTCHF platform will take time and effort, especially if the goal is to consolidate the systems. There’s also real risk around capturing revenue synergies. In theory, the cross-sell potential is compelling, but turning that into actual revenue requires the products to work well together and the right pricing. None of that happens overnight. On top of that, the all-debt financing does limit WTCHF ability to be aggressive in investments.

Conclusion

I give a buy rating for WTCHF. This is a business with strong fundamentals, a proven product, and a very clear roadmap for growth. The e2open deal does introduce some risk, but it also sets the stage for an even stronger platform. If execution stays on track, I believe WTCHF can double in value over the next three years.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Winners And Losers From The Trade War: An Equities View
data.description:
By Christian Floro, CFA, CMT, Market Strategist | Emily Foshag, CFA, Portfolio Manager, Listed Infrastructure | Lauren Choi, Portfolio Manager, Equities | Jared Meggison, CFA, Analyst, Equities | Ryan Jordan, Analyst, Equities

The U.S. is facing the largest increase in tariffs in the post-World War II era, with the aggregate impact on the economy likely to be significant.

While the temporary U.S.-China trade truce has reduced the expected tariff impact on U.S. growth and slashed recession odds, there is still likely to be some economic scarring due to shelved investment plans and the hit to both consumer and business sentiment. What’s more, overall trade tariffs are still higher compared to the start of the year, having risen from 2.3% to 12.5%, suggesting a sustained negative impact on the broader economy. The result will likely see GDP growth slowing with inflation rising, creating a challenging mix for consumer spending power and company profit margins.

Amid this period of uncertainty, tariffs will likely remain elevated, even if they settle at a slightly different level than where they are today. Protectionism is one of President Donald Trump’s long-held core beliefs, and so far, he has been able to act on it unilaterally. Moreover, tariffs are unlikely to fall below the 10% threshold, as the administration has been vocal about using tariffs as a source of revenue to support its fiscal expansion plans. As a result, an environment of elevated tariffs will continue to be an essential part of equity market analysis going forward.

Market volatility is likely to persist as trade policy remains a moving target. Indeed, while market vulnerability has declined due to the trade truce, risks remain pertinent given the still-elevated tariffs. As with any shock, there are winners and losers, along with sector and industry bifurcation.

Assessing vulnerabilities

The first step in determining individual equity market sector exposure to the impact of global trade wars is to view these effects through two distinct lenses: exposure through supply chain impacts and exposure through revenue disruptions.

The rise in U.S. import tariffs increases the cost of production for firms most reliant on international supply chains. Meanwhile, as currencies adjust or other nations retaliate against U.S. import tariffs by increasing their own import tariffs and/or instituting trade barriers, U.S. companies that derive a significant portion of their revenue abroad are also likely to be negatively impacted. Tariff repercussions are likely to be broad in scope, but they will impact each firm differently. We summarize these vulnerabilities by firm size and then by sector.

Vulnerabilities by capitalization

In aggregate, publicly traded large- and mid-cap companies across all sectors attribute nearly half of their production costs outside the U.S., leaving them particularly exposed to broad-based import tariffs. There are some nuances to consider, however. Large-cap firms appear better positioned given their bigger balance sheets, which can absorb swings in inventory, and their more diverse supply chain network, which offers some flexibility in the face of trade barriers. In addition, idiosyncratic risks stemming from sectoral tariffs appear more skewed within mid-cap companies, where over 90% of costs for both autos and pharma are attributable abroad.

Firms, on average, source 25% of their revenues from international markets, though larger firms tend to have a greater share of their revenues sourced abroad. Meanwhile, small-cap companies have the least exposure to foreign sales, with less than 20% of their revenues earned internationally. Their higher sensitivity to consumer sentiment and a potential slowdown in domestic economic activity, however, are likely to more than offset these benefits.

International share of total cost of goods sold

By sector, large-cap vs. mid-cap

Vulnerabilities by sector and industry

The impact of tariffs is likely to vary across sector groups and even more so within specific industries. This section focuses on the sectors that are likely to be most and least impacted by the ensuing tariff shock:

Technology:

Tech-oriented firms are the most exposed to international supply chain impacts and international demand-related disruptions. Over 70% of the cost of goods sold in this sector—particularly within semiconductor, capital equipment, and large-cap communication services—is sourced abroad. Moreover, the sector also has about 60% of its revenue sourced abroad.

The ongoing national security investigation by the Trump administration into the semiconductor supply chain, including manufacturing equipment and downstream products that contain semiconductors, adds significant risk. Indeed, beyond trade policy, the technology sector is likely to face elevated risks, given broader concerns about its role in national security through the exporting of critical technology. The outcome could see most global semiconductor capital equipment companies facing some degree of export restrictions to service Chinese demand.

Yet, while the outlook for tech, in general, has faced increased challenges, even those bearing the brunt of the impact—semiconductor and other tech hardware firms—have only seen minor impacts from the tariffs so far, according to their Q1 earnings reports. Helping cushion the blow has been the focus on supply chain resilience since the pandemic. Many of these firms have already reduced their dependence on China, made their supply chains more agile and flexible, and developed the ability to quickly change their sourcing. Many have also begun segmenting manufacturing to bring it closer to their end customers.

Not all tech is equally as vulnerable to trade tariffs. The software sector has a lower risk, given its smaller exposure to international supply chains and revenue, and as well as minimal direct exposure to China. This is evidenced by hyperscaler capex spending, which remains robust, suggesting there is no sign yet of uncertainty weighing on spending plans. Internet companies are also likely to be unaffected. Together with software, both are likely viewed as tech-sector safe havens given the durability of fundamentals, reasonable valuations, and minimal tariff risk.

Amid a temporary reprieve in trade hostilities and structural reasons for optimism within the space, markets have been quick to rebound, with the tech sector retracing all its losses since “liberation day.” Ultimately, however, overall sector risks remain prominent, especially given the fluid nature of trade policy, and so investors should remain vigilant of further volatility.

Materials:

This sector faces significant supply chain vulnerabilities due to the limited domestic production capacity of steel and aluminum, and because rebuilding that capacity—such as constructing new foundries—takes several years and cannot be accomplished overnight.

Risks appear to be concentrated in larger firms, with large-cap stocks sourcing nearly 80% of their production costs abroad, compared to only 40% for mid-caps. There is a size bias at play, however, as most domestically oriented firms have fallen out of large-cap status in recent years, and those remaining are primarily larger international firms.

With about 40% of the sector’s revenues sourced abroad, Materials is also particularly sensitive to global economic growth. While tail risks have eased, especially given the temporary China trade truce, global demand for commodities is likely to slow this year. The policy-induced resetting of the global trade system is bringing rise to uncertainty as countries try to navigate this new and uncomfortable equilibrium. This is likely to weigh on global economic growth and create headwinds for the sector.

International revenue exposure

By sector, percentage of total revenue, large-cap vs. mid-cap

Healthcare:

In aggregate, this sector benefits from inelastic and domestically-oriented demand. As a result, it is among the least at risk from trade policy-related revenue and supply chain disruptions, albeit with some exceptions.

In particular, tariffs still present a potential material headwind to goods-oriented industries within the space. While many have preemptively built up excess inventory ahead of the tariffs, MedTech and Life Science Tools companies, with their notable exposure to international supply chains, could be confronting an EPS headwind of around 5-10% because of the trade war.

The pharmaceutical industry also faces distinct risks, as it too is under an ongoing national security investigation, the outcome of which is highly uncertain but could potentially lead to additional tariffs or trade barriers. These trade-related risks are skewed toward large-cap pharmaceutical companies, which have nearly 70% of their supply chain costs sourced abroad. However, mid-cap companies may also be similarly, albeit indirectly, at risk. While almost 90% of mid-cap pharma production is sourced in the U.S., plenty of input materials are imported from Ireland and Switzerland. Key starting materials and active ingredients, primarily for generics, are also sourced from China and India.

Further complicating matters, as many pharma companies utilize complex transfer pricing that inflates the price of imported drugs at the border to minimize their U.S. tax burden, this unique accounting treatment would work to amplify the effects of potential tariffs.

Utilities:

These companies appear uniquely insulated to weather the uncertainty and disruption triggered by trade policy. From a supply chain perspective, most have prioritized domestic manufacturing in recent years. This is particularly true for the clean energy supply chain, as the Inflation Reduction Act and earlier trade restrictions on Chinese solar panels have incentivized domestic investments. Utility companies operate within a highly regulated business model, which limits exposure to foreign revenue exposure, and suggests that Utilities would generally be able to pass through most associated tariff cost increases to customers.

International share of total cost of goods sold

By industry group, large-cap vs. mid-cap

Financials:

Counterintuitively, despite the trade policy-induced volatility that has ensnared markets, the financial sector is also likely to be well insulated. Investment banks with high exposure to capital markets are likely to benefit the most from elevated trading volumes. Provided defaults and delinquencies remain manageable, very domestic regional banks are also poised to benefit.

Other defensive sectors:

Companies in sectors whose revenues are more domestically oriented are likely also to remain well insulated. This is likely to ring true for Consumer Staples, who stand to benefit given very inelastic demand from end-consumers. As tariffs push input prices higher, however, production and supply chain efficiencies will likely come into focus within the space, with lower-cost producers with domestically oriented production lines likely to benefit the most. Real Estate is also generally less directly exposed to tariffs, given its domestically oriented demand, but it has some vulnerabilities via higher material costs.

Investment Outlook

As with any market shock, the new U.S. tariffs will create both winners and losers, with vulnerabilities differing not just by firm size but also across sectors and industries. While some investors may retreat from tariff-exposed areas of the market, opportunities do remain.

Large-cap firms are generally less vulnerable, thanks to their diversified supply chains and ability to manage inventory swings. And even within the likely heavily impacted Tech sector, software and internet companies may offer relative safety. Meanwhile, defensive sectors like Utilities and Real Estate are less exposed due to inelastic or domestic demand. At the same time, Financials may benefit from a domestic focus, as well as potential upside stemming from market volatility.

Despite some recent reversals in trade policy, the broader trend still indicates a rise in trade barriers compared to the start of the year. In an environment that remains vulnerable to further negative economic shocks and amid ongoing uncertainty, investors should prepare for continued market volatility. Now is an opportune time to revisit diversification within equity allocations, with a focus on previously overlooked value-oriented stocks and international equities. The former exhibits hedging characteristics, given its higher exposure to financials as well as other defensive areas of the market, while the latter benefits from the increased attractiveness of Europe.

The fluid nature of trade policy means that the market backdrop will remain uncertain in the period ahead. However, beneath the surface, size, sector, style, and regional bifurcation present compelling opportunities for investors who remain disciplined and fully invested throughout the trade war.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: BXP: Near-Term Positives With A Challenged Balance Sheet
data.description:
While shares of BXP Inc (NYSE:BXP), formerly known as Boston Properties, are up 17% from last year, the stock has been on a slide since November. As we have seen long-term interest rates rise, BXP has lost over 20% of its value, given the high sensitivity of real estate valuations to interest rates. I last covered shares in June 2024, rating them a “hold.” Since then, its 13.6% rally has been a bit ahead of the market’s 10% jump, consistent with a “hold,” if not a buy. With updated financials, now is a good time to revisit BXP. I remain a hold.

BXP is a leading office REIT, primarily owning high-quality properties. It owns 185 properties with 53 million square feet, and nearly all of the properties are located in central business districts. Given its former name, it is no surprise that Boston is the most important market, but BXP also has significant exposure to other major metro markets, like New York, DC, and San Francisco.

The performance across these key markets does vary greatly. As you can see, Boston is nearly entirely leased, while West Coast cities have demonstrably lower lease rates. In general, we have seen financial services, legal services, and healthcare businesses most aggressively bring workers back into the office since COVID, whereas technology firms have taken a laxer approach. This discrepancy in leasing is largely driven by the different compositions within local economics, with the West Coast having more technology exposure. That said, in recent months, we have even seen the tech giants take a more aggressive stance against remote working, though I would expect western cities to structurally lag their eastern counterparts.

While some of the worst fears around office occupancy are unlikely to be realized, the sector is not thriving either. In the company’s first quarter on April 29th, BXP generated $1.64 of FFO, which was a penny below expectations, even as revenue grew by 3% to $865 million. Offsetting this revenue gain, expenses rose by $23 million. Funds from operations declined to $261 million from $271 million. The operating expenses of owning a building that is 80% or 90% leased are largely the same—they need an equally staffed lobby and pay the same property taxes, which is why even modest declines in occupancy can pressure margins.

That is exactly what we are seeing, BXP has an 86.9% occupancy rate. This was down 60bps sequentially. Same property occupancy is down 150bps from last year at 87.3%. This decline in occupancy has come even as its leasing activity has improved. In the last quarter, BXP successfully completed 1.1 million square feet of leasing, up 25% from last year. It aims for 4 million square feet all year, so Q1 was essentially right on budget.

To an extent, BXP’s leasing efforts are akin to running on a treadmill. Most of this leasing is merely offsetting leases that are expiring. Over the balance of this year, another 1.9 million sq ft expire. 2026 and 2027 are relatively light years, but there is a more meaningful ramp in 2028-2029. These lease rates in outer years also are a higher price. Even to the extent that BXP is able to replace tenants, it may have to do so at a lower rate.

Now, because its lease book is so well staggered with a 7.6-year average term, any decline in the business is likely to be gradual. In the very near term, I think BXP is likely to show some sequential improvement, given the light schedule of maturities. While occupancy fell 50bps sequentially, its leased rate was flat at 89.4%. When BXP leases to a new tenant, it can be a few months before they begin occupying the space as it needs to be fitted to their needs.

This occupancy to lease gap has expanded, and it should narrow back to more normalized levels in coming months, which should push occupancy back above 87%, though I expect it will be difficult to get above the 87.5% level. Management expects occupancy to be between 86.5% and 88% this year, a reasonable range, though I expect it to be below the midpoint slightly. This underpins its expectation for $6.80-$6.92 of FFO this year, down from $7.10 last year. I expect FFO of $6.80-$6.85 this year.

At this level, shares have an over 10% FFO yield, but with FFO continuing to decline, an elevated current yield is merited. While shares offer a 5.8% dividend yield, BXP has not raised its dividend since 2020. I do not expect any dividend increases either, as BXP has a $2.3 billion development pipeline, which is just 62% pre-leased. It expects a ~6.9% yield on this investment, which is unlikely to meet its cost of capital. It is also planning to develop a 46-story tower in midtown Manhattan for $2 billion.

BXP has these significant funding needs, but its balance sheet has weakened considerably. Including its share of joint ventures, BXP has $15.7 billion of debt. As debt has risen and results have declined, its leverage has increased materially, from sub-7x pre-COVID to above 8x. This increase in leverage has come even as interest rates are higher, which all else equal, argues for lower leverage. BXP needs to retain cash flow and use any incremental cash to strengthen its weak balance sheet, which leaves it vulnerable to a renewed downturn.

Now, importantly, 73% of debt is unsecured, giving BXP meaningful flexibility. Additionally, its debt is well-staggered, though fixed rate maturities will need to be rolled over at higher yields in coming years, which will apply ongoing pressure to FFO.

In the near-term, we are likely to see the business hit a bit a bottom as occupancy should pick up later this year, given its backlog of leases. Additionally, if we do see long-term interest rates pull back from their highs, that would likely support real estate shares broadly, including BXP. Still, this company has at least $1 billion of excess debt and an ambitious development pipeline with questionable returns. As such, I struggle to see a path for sustained growth in FFO beyond ~$7.00 with risk of renewed declines in the 2028-2030 period.

I believe fair value is no less than a 10% FFO yield or $65-70. I see shares at about fair value, and so I view them as a hold. I do not see a rush to sell shares, especially with some potential tactical tailwinds, though its balance sheet keeps me from being a buyer. If we saw the recent recovery continue towards $75, I would be a seller. BXP is a solid operator in a challenged sector, and as such, I believe shares are appropriately valued.

data.title: Boston Scientific: Old, Reliable; Buy For Their Consistent Growth
data.description:
The medical device industry sits in the heart of delivering promising healthcare results, while implementing advanced technology and engineering. Minimally invasive surgical tools, wearable technologies, and surging robotic capabilities are leading the way. There are a few standout companies by size in the industry, although each tends to specialize in a specific expertise.

Boston Scientific (NYSE:BSX) is one of those established firms. The company develops and manufactures medical devices used across interventional medical specialties. BSX works in two segments, MedSurg and Cardiovascular, with the latter producing about 65% of net sales. Gastrointestinal, urological, neurological, artery, and cardiac conditions are a few of the circumstances where BSX has industry-leading devices aiding in diagnosis and treatment.

I want to showcase the long-term chart for BSX, as the firm is up nearly 500% in the last decade. You can see the consistency in steady gains over time, with really the only two major corrections coming from the pandemic in 2020, and the trade tensions earlier this year, which they already have fully rebounded from. BSX is a global, dependable leader in the healthcare equipment industry, and I rate this stock a buy. Q1 earnings were a win with growth across every segment. Earnings continue to rise, and guidance remains above the industry. BSX is growing organically and through acquisition and may not be as overvalued as the numbers portray.

Q1 Sales

Q1 earnings were out at the end of April and overall were a win for BSX. Net sales increased YoY in both MedSurg and Cardiovascular, as well as every sub-segment within. Cardiology, their largest sub-segment, gained 29.8% in global revenue, and 44.6% in the U.S. alone. Electrophysiology was the biggest uptick, with 143.2% higher sales than Q1 last year. As a firm, global net sales were 21% higher YoY, reaching a record quarterly revenue of $4.66 billion.

Adjusted operating margin (non-GAAP) was 33.1% for MedSurg and 33.6% for Cardiovascular in Q1. 2024 full year adjusted operating margin was 27.0%. Q1 has started on the right foot for BSX.

Guidance is not expected to be as robust as Q1 has been but is still very optimistic. Operational and organic growth are still anticipated to climb between 12.0-17.5% total for full year 2025. Even with lower sales than Q1, this is still a strong yearly projection and a positive for BSX.

Q1 Earnings

EPS in the first quarter for BSX was $0.75 beating expectations by $.08 and also their highest ever for any quarter. Earnings growth rate for BSX has been around 17.6%, and future EPS is predicted 15.8% for 2025. I am not pessimistic about this dropping, as it is still healthy progress, and is ahead of the industry average EPS expected growth of 12.5%.

Adjusted EPS was 47% higher in 2024 from just two years ago, and Zacks EPS revisions have grown by 2.2% in just the last month.

Organic and Acquisition Growth

BSX has had success growing organically and through acquisitions. This ability is a strength for the medical device manufacturer. Organic revenue has now grown 14.4% YoY over the prior two years and has been positive in every quarter for the last four years.

The company has completed 55 total acquisitions, on average for around $500 million per transaction. The companies acquired also hail from 8 different countries, showcasing the firm’s diverse portfolio. The most recent being the purchase of SoniVie, a company that has developed a system for denervation of renal nerves around blood vessels for treating hypertensive disorders. BSX currently holds a 10% stake in SoniVie, and this is how many of their acquisitions play out. They have an active venture capital portfolio, which currently has 45 investments. This is typically where they target their transactions as they are already familiar with these companies.

Peer Comparison

There are a few stalwarts that primarily run the healthcare equipment world. Intuitive Surgical (ISRG) and BSX have led the pack in return by a good margin in the last 5 years.

Profitability is a strong suit for the industry, and BSX barely leads the group with a gross profit margin of 68.45%. BSX and ISRG have a much greater revenue growth than the others, but they also have noticeably higher valuation. ISRG is more specifically focused on surgical robotics and may be the leader in that niche. This is not a knock on ISRG, but I like the wide product portfolio for BSX, which does include robotic assisted devices as well.

Risk

The biggest risk with BSX is its valuation. With a trailing P/E of 77x, it’s clear the stock could be overvalued. Price/Book and Price/Sales are both also over 2x the industry median.

If you look at the P/E ratio over the last 3 years, they are actually sitting in the middle to lower half of the average. In fact, exactly 3 years ago, BSX had a P/E of 76x. Since then, the stock has climbed from $40 a share to over $104.

Other risks include competition in the market as healthcare equipment startups are trying to emerge. BSX has a broad enough portfolio across their segments, and with almost 50 years of business, is not going to get outpaced, especially in the cardiovascular space.

Conclusion

Profitability is fantastic for BSX, with a 3x industry average EBITDA margin of 25.84%. Sales and operating income have consistently grown each of the past 5 years. 2024 operating expenses were 35.7% of net sales, down from 36.4% in 2023. Earnings growth has been steady, and I really like their ability to grow organically and through successful acquisitions. Since the stock's bottom in 2012 at $5 a share, the company has hardly looked back and has provided about as stable growth as you can find. I don’t expect that to stop, buy BSX.

data.title: DoorDash Has All The Makings Of A Long-Term Compounder
data.description:
In the past, we’ve written countless articles about Compounders - stocks that we think could deliver higher-than-average returns for longer-than-average periods of time.

Typically, we focus on a couple of things when it comes to identifying these 'Compounders' - namely, a history of boosting revenues and net income, along with consistent decreases in the outstanding share count.

This is due to our belief that stocks don’t 'magically' appreciate; it requires positive developments in both the supply and demand dynamics of a stock in order to increase a company's share price over time.

Another thing that we often look for is a track record.

This is because firms with a strong track record tend to have considerable inertia, such that dethroning them or dislodging them from their advantaged market position would take years and considerable capital. Thus, a strong track record - to us - shows a firm that is likely to keep winning.

In this article, we wanted to approach the idea of investing in stocks like this from earlier in the cycle. All of the compounders that we’ve covered were just regular companies at some point, until they found their groove, notching consistent increases in demand for shares while simultaneously decreasing the outstanding supply.

What do these companies look like before they appreciate massively?

It’s not a perfect science, but we would argue that DoorDash (NASDAQ:DASH) has all the makings of a future compounder.

The company has managed to grow revenues like clockwork while simultaneously boosting margins and bottom-line profits. These margin improvements should eventually lead to a reversal in the company's share count, which has up been increasing up until now.

As DASH continues to expand into global markets and capitalize on its duopoly position in delivery, we see considerable appreciation potential for shareholders going forward.

Today, we'll take a much deeper look at DASH, explain our thesis, and make the case that this underrated stock could be one of the markets next great compounders.

Sound good? Let’s dive in.

DoorDash's Financials

As we mentioned at the start, when it comes to defining a 'Compounder', we look for three things: increasing revenue, increasing net income, and decreasing supply of outstanding shares.

We've found that these elements - more than anything else - are responsible for a lion's share of stock appreciation in the market today.

Thankfully, when it comes to DASH, the company has done an excellent job some - if not all - of these fronts.

First, when it comes to revenues, DASH has shown incredible consistency in increasing the scope and size of the business, growing revenues from only $2.85 billion in January 2021, to more than $11.2 billion over the last 12 months:

This is high growth, and the result of a smart expansion strategy from management.

As we see it, there’s considerable demand for cheap delivery of all types of products, including food orders, grocery, alcohol, convenience items, flowers, and more.

Thus, expanding the business is simply a matter of executing on bringing DoorDash’s offerings to more consumers in more places, and building that flywheel.

In addition to organic growth from the flagship brand, DoorDash has also acquired a number of global competitors, including Wolt, Deliveroo, Caviar, and SevenRooms. These acquisitions have increased both the horizontal and vertical scale of DASH, and the company has grown into multiple new geographies and categories as a result.

Now, as the delivery market has consolidated into only a few major players globally, we think DASH stands to see continued growth, with lower accompanying in operational unit costs. This lack of competition and heightened operating leverage should boost DASH's net incomes at a pace even faster than top-line growth, which would be a huge boon to shareholders.

Over the last two years, DASH has improved net income results, from nearly $1.4 billion in losses at the start of 2023, to now $339 million in profit over the last 12 months. This growth is due to the company's strong operating leverage and competitive position, which we highlighted before.

Looking forward, we expect that DASH's currently slim margins could expand to the 20 to 25% range as the firm gets larger and larger. DoorDash's gross margins have grown towards the ~50% mark, meaning there’s no logical reason why DoorDash couldn’t eventually reach net margins in this area.

When combined with our expectations for 20%+ YoY top-line growth in both of the coming years, we think DoorDash is set to substantially increase demand for company shares in the years ahead.

On the supply side, DASH heavily subsidizes operating costs with stock-based compensation, which currently totals more than $1 billion per year. This is a serious non-cash expense, which has come at the cost of progress on EPS:

In the past, DASH has had to use equity to subsidize operating costs. However, as the company reaches true GAAP profitability, we expect that this lavish spending on SBC should decrease, and future buybacks could eventually reverse this dynamic. Within a few years, we could see all of DASH's top line, bottom line, and share count numbers moving in the 'right' direction.

This positions the firm as being exactly what we look for.

Valuation

Of course, no investment thesis is complete without discussing valuation, which can often disqualify positions that we might otherwise find interesting.

Thankfully, we think the valuation isn't an issue in the case of DASH.

First - we're not crazy. We know that right now, DASH's valuation looks stretched. With a price to sales ratio of 7.5x and a price to earnings ratio of 264x the stock's valuation earns an 'F' rating from Seeking Alpha's Quant Rating System, which has been historically good at picking winning stocks:

That’s a fair analysis of where things currently sit.

However, if you look at the company over a longer time horizon, we’d argue that the stock appears incredibly cheap. If you use the base case 2028 estimates that analysts have cooked up, then you could end up with a 2028 PE of roughly 20x to 22x. Again, the stock is expensive, but the growth opportunity here - especially now that competition appears more limited - is also very substantial. 20-22x seems 'ok' in our book.

Viewed differently, at present, DASH trades at a market cap of roughly $86 billion, which some might find to be expensive.

That said, if you use our projections from before, then DASH could produce $20 billion (or more) in revenue by 2028, along with $5 billion (or more) in net income. This calculation utilizes the higher net income margins we're anticipating will materialize between now and then. For a company that could produce 5 billion in earnings within the next 3 to 4 years, we think 17.2x earnings appears very reasonable.

Overall, we think the combination of DASH's organic performance, growth trajectory, and present valuation make it look like an attractive, 'Strong Buy' in the current market.

Risk

Of course, there are risks when it comes to investing in DASH, and it’s worth outlining some of those now.

First off, while we mention that DASH's valuation appears to be a reasonable price to pay (when viewed over the long term), at present the stock is expensive. In a market selloff, stocks with high multiples are always the first to see their shares trimmed, and we expect the same would be true for DASH.

Additionally, in the event that the tariff situation gets worse, GDP growth slows, or consumer health deteriorates, we’d expect DASH shares to get pummeled.

Similarly – DASH has considerable execution risk going forward with respect to its Wolt and Deliveroo acquisitions. Figuring out what to do with the established brand equity in both of these companies will be a challenge, and there's no established playbook in the market for ensuring maximum absorption of value.

We think DASH will be able to manage this risk, but the company has spent big to boost scale, and it needs to ensure it gets its money's worth from those transactions.

Summary

Overall, we see DoorDash as a potential compounder that could produce serious appreciation for investors over the long haul.

Compounders are never cheap, and investing in them isn’t without risk, but we'd argue that DoorDash's potential growth, financial strength, strong brand equity, and limited competition make the stock 'Strong Buy' for the years ahead.

Stay safe out there!

data.title: John Hancock Regional Bank Fund Q1 2025 Commentary
data.description:
Highlights

  • The fund's secondary benchmark-the S&P Regional Banks Select Industry Index-returned - 5.16% in the first quarter.
  • The regional banking sector trailed the broader market, as measured by the -4.27% return for the S&P 500 Index. (SP500), (SPX)
  • The John Hancock Regional Bank Fund (MUTF:FRBAX) marginally underperformed its benchmark.

Market review and outlook

After performing well in January, regional bank stocks turned lower in February and early March to finish the quarter in negative territory. The initial gains reflected upbeat sentiment at the start of the year. The economy continued to grow, helped by a decline in initial jobless claims, relatively low unemployment, and positive personal consumption trends. Mounting expectations for deregulation under the new administration further aided returns. However, the sector subsequently lost ground as concerns about the economic impact of the protectionist shift in U.S. trade policy fueled a rotation into more defensive sectors.

Although bank stocks experienced volatility in tandem with the overall market, banks entered the period of uncertainty with robust fundamentals. Banks continued to exhibit solid profitability supported by strong capital and liquidity, and credit costs remained low. In addition, deposit rates declined as the U.S. Federal Reserve lowered interest rates. Investors also displayed optimism that a potential shift toward a more favorable regulatory environment could lead to increased merger-and-acquisition activity and spark an uptick in loan demand in the latter half of 2025.

Despite persistent headlines about commercial real estate, most banks' underwriting continued to be conservative, and their credit costs remained benign. The average small- to mid-sized bank reported quarterly net charge-offs of only 13 basis points ( 0.13 percentage points), a historically low level.

As per our research, most banks expect net interest margins will grow in 2025 as fixed-rate assets continue to reprice higher. This means that the portion of loan books originated during the low-rate environment of 2020-2022 will mature and be replaced with much higher-rate loans over the next few years, creating a tailwind for revenues. We believe net interest margin expansion, coupled with loan growth, should drive healthy revenue gains in 2025 . Regional banks traded at 1.07 times book value and approximately 10.7 times earnings estimates as of March 31. Both represented sizable discounts relative to history and the market as a whole.

Contributors and detractors

An overweight in Coastal Financial Corp. (CCB), which displayed continued momentum with strong fourth-quarter earnings results in late January, was the top contributor to relative performance. The company is in the process of developing five new fintech partnerships for which it has recently signed letters of intent. We believe these partnerships will leverage the capital the bank raised last year. An out-of-benchmark holding in JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM) also contributed positively after reporting solid results and offering an upbeat outlook. Management also boosted its net interest revenue guidance, as the recent steepening of the yield curve was expected to create more favorable operating conditions. Overweights in Nicolet Bankshares, Inc. (NIC) and Landmark Bancorp, Inc. (LARK) further helped relative performance.

An overweight in U.S. Bancorp (USB), which isn't represented in the index, was the largest detractor. Investors were disappointed by weaker fee revenue from the bank's payments business, and the bank surprised investors by announcing a sooner-than-expected transition to a new chief executive officer. Overweights in OP Bancorp (OPBK) and Live Oak Bancshares, Inc. (LOB) also detracted.

Portfolio changes

Although we didn't make any major changes to the fund during the quarter, we eliminated one holding due to concerns about strategic actions implemented by its management team, and we significantly reduced another given uncertainty regarding the local economy in its core market. We rotated the proceeds into existing positions.

What you should know before investing

A portfolio concentrated in one sector or that holds a limited number of securities may fluctuate more than a diversified portfolio. The stock prices of midsize and small companies can change more frequently and dramatically than those of large companies. Foreign investing, especially in emerging markets, has additional risks, such as currency and market volatility and political and social instability. Hedging and other strategic transactions may increase volatility and result in losses if not successful.
